**Bridge to §E.** Slide 38 is the validation diagnostic for the AE-as-representation. Slide 41 is the *deployment* form of the same idea — except now we *use* the per-frame MSE distribution as an anomaly score. Same number, different role.
:::

## 39. Bottleneck Visualisation Preview

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The 2-D scatter**

- Train AE with $d = 2$ (or project larger $d$ to 2D via PCA).
- Plot every micrograph as a point in $z$-space.
- Colour by *known* property (phase, hardness, anneal time) if available.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What you typically see**

- Smooth gradients along property axes.
- Clusters separated by *content*, not session.
- Trajectories along processing variables.
- This is the **W9 latent-space view** in embryonic form.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
> Tease for W9: t-SNE and UMAP take this picture much further; contrastive learning sharpens the geometry; the AE bottleneck of today is the simplest representative of that family.
:::

::: {.notes}
**The single best slide of the unit, in my opinion.** This is where students *see* a learned representation for the first time. Six weeks ago they were doing PCA; four weeks ago they were doing CNNs; today they have a 2D map of an entire dataset where neighbourhoods correspond to similar *materials*. That visualisation is the bridge between abstract algorithm and concrete materials physics.

**When to use $d=2$ vs PCA-of-$d=32$.** $d=2$ AE is interpretable but underfits — the bottleneck is too tight for any non-trivial dataset. $d=32$ AE + PCA-to-2 visualises the *useful* representation rather than a degraded one. Default is the second, with the first only for didactic visualisation.

**Anti-pattern: t-SNE on raw images.** Computes pairwise distances on raw pixel space — meaningless for materials data. t-SNE on $z$-space (the AE bottleneck) is meaningful. Always: representation first, visualisation second.

**The anti-cluster.** If the 2D scatter shows a *continuum* — long curve, no discrete groups — that is *not* a failure. It is the dataset *telling you* it has no discrete clusters, just a continuous variation. Use the AE coordinate to predict properties (slide 37); do not force K-means on it.

**Forward link, named.** "In Unit 9 we will use t-SNE / UMAP / contrastive learning to make this scatter much sharper. The geometry of the bottleneck is the *subject* of W9. Today we just trained an encoder; W9 asks what the encoder *learned* about the dataset."
:::

# §E · AE-Based Anomaly Detection {.section}

## 40. The Anomaly-Detection Setup

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The premise**

- Train an AE *only on nominal data* — frames / signals known to be "good".
- The AE learns to compress nominal data accurately.
- Anomalous data — by definition not in the training distribution — *cannot* be compressed accurately.
- Reconstruction error $\to$ anomaly score.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Operational form**

- Score: $a(x) = \|x - g_\theta(f_\theta(x))\|^2$.
- High $a$: anomaly.
- Threshold $\tau$: from nominal *validation* error distribution (not from anomaly examples).
- Flag if $a(x) > \tau$.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
**The conceptual leap of §E.** Until now we used the AE to *represent* data ($z$, slide 39) or to *reconstruct* clean data (slide 34). Section E uses the *failure mode* of the AE — its inability to reconstruct out-of-distribution input — as the actual signal. The bug is the feature.

**Why this is uniquely well-suited to materials anomaly detection.**

- **Anomalies are diverse and rare.** A supervised classifier needs labels for *every type* of anomaly; AE anomaly detection needs labels for *no* anomalies, just a clean nominal training set.
- **Anomalies are unknown unknowns.** Many failure modes in microscopy / process monitoring (a new contamination type, a never-before-seen defect) wouldn't be in any training set. The AE flags them simply because they don't compress.
- **Localisation is free.** The per-pixel residual map shows *where* in the image the anomaly lives — a critical operational requirement.

**Anti-pattern that ruins the deployment.** Including any anomaly in the training set. The AE will learn to compress that anomaly type and stop flagging it. Even one frame of (say) carbon contamination in 10000 nominal frames can teach the AE to reconstruct contamination accurately. *Strict nominal-only curation* is the entire engineering challenge.

**Notation cross-check.** The score $a(x)$ uses the same MSE loss as training. Some implementations use SSIM-based or perceptual scores instead — sharper for some anomaly types. Mention; do not derive today.
:::

## 41. Anomaly Score: Frame-Level vs Pixel-Level

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Frame-level**

$$a(x) = \tfrac{1}{HW} \sum_{i,j} (x_{ij} - \hat{x}_{ij})^2$$

- One scalar per frame.
- Use case: "is this frame anomalous?"
- Drives *frame-level* decisions (keep / flag / reject).
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Pixel-level (residual map)**

$$r_{ij}(x) = (x_{ij} - \hat{x}_{ij})^2$$

- A 2-D image, same shape as input.
- Use case: "*where* in this frame is the anomaly?"
- Drives *spatial* decisions (which region to re-image, ROI selection).
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
**Use both.** Frame-level for *triage* ("is anything wrong?"); pixel-level for *localisation* ("show me what's wrong"). The frame-level score is the average of the pixel-level residual; you get the pixel map free as a by-product of computing the score.

**Pixel-level smoothing.** Raw per-pixel residual is noisy — even nominal frames have non-zero residual due to AE reconstruction error. A small Gaussian blur (~3 px) on the residual map suppresses noise and surfaces *spatially coherent* anomalies. This is the operational form used in industrial deployments.

**Threshold on pixel maps.** A single global threshold on $r_{ij}$ is rarely right; per-pixel thresholds (calibrated from per-pixel statistics on nominal validation data) handle non-uniform sensor response, vignetting, etc. For a first deployment, global threshold is fine; for production, calibrate per pixel.

**Frame-level pitfall.** A small but severe local anomaly (a one-pixel-wide bright defect) is *averaged out* in the frame-level score. Always inspect the *max* of the residual map alongside the mean — it is more sensitive to localised anomalies. In practice we use both: $\bar{r} > \tau_{\rm mean}$ OR $\max_{ij} r_{ij} > \tau_{\rm max}$.

**Forward link.** This per-pixel residual approach generalises to volumetric (3D) and time-series data trivially: replace the AE with a 3D-conv-AE or a 1D-conv-AE / sequence model. Same logic.
:::

## 42. Threshold Selection from Nominal Validation

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The procedure**

1. Hold out a nominal validation set $\mathcal{V}_{\rm nom}$ (no anomalies).
2. Compute $\{a(x) : x \in \mathcal{V}_{\rm nom}\}$.
3. Pick a high quantile (95%, 99%, 99.9%) as $\tau$.
4. Deploy: flag frames with $a > \tau$.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The cardinal rule**

- **Never** pick $\tau$ from anomaly examples.
- That would be supervised threshold tuning ⇒ leakage of anomaly labels into the detector.
- The threshold must be *purely a property of nominal data*.
- Anomalies are evaluated *after* deployment as a sanity check.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
**Repeat the rule three times in lecture.** Threshold from nominal. Threshold from nominal. Threshold from nominal. This is the most-violated rule in industrial anomaly detection — and the one that makes deployments quietly fail. A threshold tuned on a few labelled anomalies overfits to *those* anomalies and fails on the next batch of unknown unknowns.

**Why a quantile, not a mean.** The nominal score distribution is typically heavy-tailed even on clean data. A mean + 3$\sigma$ threshold misses the tail; a 99.9% quantile catches it. Use quantiles.

**Calibrating the false-positive rate.** $\tau$ at 99% quantile of nominal $\Rightarrow$ 1% nominal frames falsely flagged. For deployment at 1000 frames per session, that's 10 nominal frames flagged per session — manageable as a human-review burden. Tune the quantile to match the operational human-in-the-loop budget. There is no universally correct quantile; it is a deployment trade-off.

**Reporting in a paper.** Report the ROC curve (TPR vs FPR across thresholds) — only feasible if a labelled anomaly test set exists for *evaluation*. The threshold *itself* still comes from the nominal validation set; the labelled anomaly set tells you what TPR / FPR your threshold delivered. Two distinct sets, two distinct roles.

**Practical exception.** When *prior knowledge* of the expected false-positive rate exists (e.g., regulatory: max 0.1% missed defects), set the quantile from that requirement and report what TPR results.
:::

## 43. Case Study — Melt-Pool Monitoring (LPBF, 1/2)

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Process**

- **Laser powder-bed fusion (LPBF):** metal additive manufacturing.
- A laser melts a powder bed layer-by-layer; melt-pool dynamics determine porosity, surface quality, and microstructure.
- Co-axial high-speed thermal camera captures the melt-pool at $\sim 10$ kHz.
- Hours of build $\to 10^7$+ frames per part.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The anomaly-detection task**

- Anomalies: pore-formation events, keyhole transitions, lack-of-fusion regions.
- Detect them in real time, on a single GPU at the print station.
- A frame-level score $a(x)$ above $\tau$ → flag the layer for post-build inspection.
- Train AE only on a "nominal" build with low porosity (verified post-mortem by CT).
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
**Why LPBF is the canonical anomaly-detection case study.** (1) High data rate — a perfect ML use case. (2) Anomalies have clear physical correlates (pores, lack-of-fusion). (3) Ground truth via post-mortem CT enables retrospective evaluation. (4) Industrial demand: parts certification depends on anomaly detection.

**Train-set curation challenge.** "Nominal" builds with zero pores are rare and expensive to verify. Pragmatic compromise: train on builds with porosity *below* a threshold (say, $<0.01$%), verified by representative CT. Some weak training-set contamination is tolerated; the AE's bottleneck still concentrates on the dominant nominal mode.

**The 10-kHz constraint.** AE inference must be sub-millisecond. A small conv-AE (~250k params) on a single modern GPU does this comfortably; a U-Net or Transformer would not. This is one of the few deep-learning use cases where *small* AEs are the *correct* engineering choice.

**Pre-empt.** "What about supervised classification with porosity labels?" Answer: porosity labels are *post-mortem* and *aggregate* (volume fraction per layer, not per frame). Per-frame supervised labels do not exist. AE anomaly detection is the right tool because the supervision is unavailable.

**Forward link to Unit 7.** Time-series and process-monitoring methods (recurrent AEs, transformers on melt-pool sequences) extend this further. Today: per-frame AE; Unit 7: per-sequence model.
:::

## 44. Case Study — Melt-Pool Monitoring (LPBF, 2/2)

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Result**

- AE flags candidate frames per layer.
- Post-mortem CT identifies pore locations in $(x, y, z)$.
- Spatial correlation: AE-flagged regions overlap with CT-detected pores at $\sim 80$+ % localisation accuracy.
- Layer-level decisions: re-melt, accept, or reject.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Operational integration**

- **Per-frame:** sub-ms inference, decision logged.
- **Per-layer:** aggregate residual map → spatial heatmap of anomaly density.
- **Per-build:** statistical control charts on anomaly counts → alert on drift.
- Closed loop with the laser controller is a live research direction (Unit 7 / 13).
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
**This is real, not hypothetical.** Several industrial LPBF platforms (EOS, SLM Solutions, GE Additive, plus academic partnerships including Pelz-group collaborators) deploy in-situ melt-pool monitoring with anomaly detection in production. The architecture is essentially the slide-43 setup; the engineering is making it deployable at print speed across part geometries.

**Operational layers — three distinct timescales.**

- *ms scale (per-frame):* AE flags individual events. Volume of data is enormous; only flagged frames are stored.
- *minute scale (per-layer):* anomaly density map across the layer. Drives re-melt decisions.
- *hour scale (per-build):* control charts of layer-level statistics. Drives recipe-tuning decisions.

Each layer of the hierarchy has its own threshold, calibrated on its own nominal-validation-set quantile. Flag-fatigue is avoided by aggregating from the bottom up.

**Failure modes to mention.**

- **Sensor drift:** thermal camera response shifts over hours of operation. Periodic re-calibration on a known-good area, or AE re-fitting on rolling windows.
- **Geometry-dependent nominal:** a thin-wall feature has different nominal melt-pool dynamics than a bulk region. Geometry-conditional AE (input includes a CAD-derived feature) is the modern fix.

**Forward link to W13.** Physics-informed ML (W13) embeds the melt-pool physics directly into the AE loss — penalising reconstructions that violate energy balance or thermal-diffusion physics. The result is far more sensitive anomaly detection. Mention by name.
:::

## 45. Case Study — SEM Long-Run Anomaly Flagging

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Process**

- Automated SEM session: 4–48 h, 10 000–100 000 frames.
- Operator absent overnight.
- Failure modes mid-session: focus drift, charging, contamination, beam shutter issue, sample tilt.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The AE pipeline**

- Train AE on a known-good session with the *same instrument and sample type*.
- Per-frame reconstruction error in real time.
- Real-time alerts to the operator's phone for high-anomaly clusters.
- Post-session: histogram of anomaly counts per acquisition area for triage.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
**The economic argument.** A typical automated SEM session costs the lab $\sim$\$500/h in instrument time and operator overhead. Drift-induced failure of an overnight session loses 12 h of beam time *and* the data — easily \$5000 per incident. AE anomaly detection at the cost of a one-time small-AE training run pays for itself in a single avoided incident. Make this point aloud — it is the strongest argument for unsupervised methods in any materials lab.

**Distinct from §B's K-means quality gate (slide 20).** Both serve as quality control. K-means clustering catches anomalies that fall *outside the embedded nominal cluster* — anomalies *of kind*. AE catches anomalies that fall *outside the AE's learned manifold* — anomalies *of appearance*. Use both in parallel; they catch different things.

**Practical instrument-conditional training.** Train one AE per instrument-sample combination. SEM AE for the Hitachi at 5 kV on steel is *not* the same as SEM AE for the Zeiss at 30 kV on Cu. Same algorithm; different weights. Plan for $\sim$5–10 deployed AEs in a working materials lab.

**Operator UI matters more than the AE.** Even a perfect anomaly detector is useless if the operator can't act on its alerts. Engineering: dashboard with last 50 alerted frames + reconstruction residual map + suggested action. The slide is one line; the engineering is multi-week.

**Bridge to Unit 11.** Automation in microscopy (Unit 11) is the full deployment story; this slide is the anomaly-detection module of that broader picture. Forward-pointer named.
:::

## 46. Failure Mode 1 — Training Set Too Narrow

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The trap**

- AE trained on a single sample, single magnification, single brightness.
- Deployed to slightly different conditions (a sample with a rare-but-real microstructure).
- AE flags the rare-but-real frames as anomalies.
- High false-positive rate $\to$ alert fatigue $\to$ operators silence the system.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Mitigation**

- Train on *broad* nominal data: many sessions, samples, magnifications, brightnesses.
- Augment: brightness/contrast jitter, small rotations, additive Gaussian noise.
- Periodically *re-train* the AE on a sliding window of recent nominal data.
- Track false-positive rate per session; alert on its drift.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
**The single most common deployment failure.** "We trained the AE for a week, deployed it, it flagged 30% of frames as anomalies on day one, the operators turned it off, and now no one uses it." This is the modal trajectory of an unmonitored anomaly-detection system.

**The fix is process, not algorithm.** No AE architecture saves you from a too-narrow training set. The fix is a *data-curation discipline*: actively collect nominal data across the full operating envelope. Plan a "nominal harvest" phase as long or longer than the AE training itself.

**A concrete number.** A useful conv-AE for SEM anomaly detection typically needs $\sim$ 10 000+ nominal frames spanning $\geq 5$ sessions, $\geq 3$ samples, and the operating-magnification range you actually use. This is roughly a one-week dedicated harvest at any active microscopy lab.

**The drift problem.** Even a well-curated AE drifts: the instrument ages, the operators rotate, samples shift in style. A static AE deployed for a year will accumulate false positives. Schedule a quarterly re-training on the most recent quarter's nominal data; track and report the AE's FPR over time as a maintenance metric.

**Operational test for "broad enough".** After training, sample 1000 random frames from production data. If the AE flags more than (say) 5%, the training set was too narrow — augment, retrain, repeat. This is the deployment-readiness check.
:::

## 47. Failure Mode 2 — Anomaly Leakage into Training

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The trap**

- Training set claimed to be nominal but actually contains anomalies.
- AE *learns to compress* the leaked anomaly type.
- That anomaly type is *no longer detected* at deployment.
- False-negative rate climbs silently.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Mitigation**

- Strict nominal-only curation: visual review or independent QA pass.
- Hold-out *anomaly* validation set with hand-labelled examples — *not* used for training, *only* for evaluation.
- Track recall on this anomaly set after every retraining.
- If recall drops, audit recent training data for leakage.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
**The harder of the two failure modes.** Slide 46 (training too narrow) reveals itself loudly via false positives. Slide 47 (anomaly leakage) is *silent* — the system continues to operate, missing the anomaly types that leaked. You cannot detect this from internal AE statistics; you need an external anomaly-labelled evaluation set.

**The two-set discipline.**

- *Training set:* nominal-only, strict. Used for AE optimisation. No anomaly labels needed.
- *Anomaly evaluation set:* hand-labelled anomalies (small but diverse). *Never* used for training or threshold tuning. *Only* used to compute the recall the system delivers.

This is the only known way to detect anomaly-leakage failures.

**Why the discipline gets violated.** Hand-labelling anomalies is expensive; the temptation is to use them for training-set sanity checks ("AE flags these correctly, ship it"). Resist. Once an anomaly example is touched during training-set curation, it's contaminated as a held-out evaluation example.

**A common version of this in LPBF.** "Nominal" builds verified by CT may have sub-CT-resolution pores that the AE learns to compress. Sub-CT pores then go undetected at deployment. Mitigation: pixel-level rather than frame-level scoring (slide 41), since localised sub-CT pores still produce localised AE residuals.

**Recap of slides 46–47, said aloud at the end.** "False positives = training set too narrow. False negatives = anomalies in training set. Both kill deployments. Both are *data* problems, not *algorithm* problems."
:::

# §F · Wrap-Up {.section}

## 48. Method-vs-Application Matrix

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="100%"}

| Method \\ Application | Phase ID | Denoising | Anomaly | Compression | Downstream regression |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| §A Classical clustering (K-means / GMM) | $\checkmark\checkmark$ |  | $\checkmark$ |  |  |
| §B CNN-embedding clustering | $\checkmark\checkmark$ |  | $\checkmark$ |  | $\checkmark$ |
| §C Hyperspectral clustering | $\checkmark\checkmark$ |  |  |  |  |
| §D Conv-AE | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark\checkmark$ |  | $\checkmark\checkmark$ | $\checkmark\checkmark$ |
| §E AE anomaly detection |  |  | $\checkmark\checkmark$ |  |  |

:::
:::

::: {.notes}
**Read this matrix aloud, slowly.** The single takeaway: *no method is universal*. Each one has a sweet-spot application; using a method outside its sweet-spot is the most common form of "ML doesn't work for materials" complaint.

**Highlights.**

- **Phase ID:** clustering is the natural tool. Hand-crafted features (§A) when interpretability matters; CNN embeddings (§B) when image content does.
- **Denoising:** AE is the natural tool — a *clean* AE input is the AE's job description. Clustering doesn't help.
- **Anomaly detection:** AE wins decisively (§E). Clustering can flag outliers via singletons (slide 19) but is far less sensitive.
- **Compression:** AE only. Clustering is discrete by definition; no information for reconstruction.
- **Downstream regression:** AE features (slide 37) win when bulk unlabeled data is available; CNN embeddings (slide 17) win when ImageNet transfer is good enough; classical features (§A) win when the descriptor is itself physically meaningful.

**Exam-ready summary.** Given a materials problem, pick the method by *what you want out*: cluster ID, clean output, anomaly score, compressed representation, or downstream prediction. Then pick the feature space (hand-crafted, CNN embedding, hyperspectral, AE bottleneck) by *what kind of data you have*.

**The matrix is *not* exhaustive.** Spectral unmixing, contrastive embeddings, VAE / diffusion all extend it. They are the next several units of the course. Today's matrix is the foundation; later units fill more cells.
:::

## 49. Forward Pointers — The Bottleneck Through-Line

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**W9 — Latent-space depth**

- Reframe the AE bottleneck as a *manifold*.
- Visualisation: t-SNE, UMAP.
- Better encoders: contrastive learning (SimCLR, DINO), masked autoencoders.
- Same $z$ — sharper geometry.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**W11 — Generative AE use**

- Same encoder–decoder topology, but $z$ is *regularised* to a known distribution.
- Variational autoencoder (VAE), diffusion models.
- Sampling new microstructures: $z \sim p(z) \to g_\theta(z) = $ new image.
- Inverse design and process-map generation.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
> **The single sentence to leave with:** *the autoencoder bottleneck $z$ is the through-line of the second half of the semester.* W5 trains it; W9 visualises and sharpens it; W11 samples from it.
:::

::: {.notes}
**Repeat the through-line.** I said it at slide 02 and slide 29; I say it again at slide 49 because *this is the lecture-level summary*. The bottleneck is the conceptual object that ties the second half of the course together.

**Why this through-line matters pedagogically.** Many courses present unsupervised methods as a grab-bag (clustering here, AE there, t-SNE later, VAE separately). The through-line lets students *organise* what would otherwise feel like disconnected algorithms. K-means is a discrete partition of $z$-space. t-SNE is a 2-D visualisation of $z$-space. VAE is a probabilistic structuring of $z$-space. Same object; different uses.

**Wrap with one sentence per future unit.**

- W6 (transfer learning): how to use *labelled* data to refine $z$.
- W7 (time series): how to extend $z$ to sequential / temporal data.
- W9 (latent-space depth): how to make $z$-geometry sharper.
- W11 (generative): how to sample from $z$.
- W12 (uncertainty): how to put error bars on $z$-based predictions.
- W13 (PINN): how to embed physics into $z$.

The bottleneck — today, May 2026 — is the prerequisite for every one of these.

**Final exhortation aloud.** "If you take one thing away from today: representation learning is the *centre* of modern ML, not the periphery. Clustering and AEs are how you get there with no labels. The next three months show you what you do once you're there."
:::

## 50. Exercise + Reading Assignment

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Exercise (90 min, this afternoon)**

1. **K-means on grain features.** Implement Lloyd's iteration from scratch on a 2-D synthetic descriptor set. Compare to `sklearn.cluster.KMeans` and `sklearn.mixture.GaussianMixture`. Choose $k$ via silhouette and BIC.
2. **Conv-AE for denoising.** Train a small conv-AE on noisy synthetic micrographs. Report PSNR + SSIM vs a Gaussian-filter baseline.
3. **Bonus: AE anomaly detection.** Score a test set with planted anomalies; plot ROC; pick a threshold from nominal validation quantiles.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Reading for next week**

- **@sandfeld_materials_data_science**, Ch 11 (clustering for materials data) and Ch 19 sections on AE / microscopy case studies.
- **@goodfellow2016deep**, Ch 14 (autoencoders) — sections 14.1–14.3 are sufficient.
- **@bishop2006pattern** §9.1–9.2 if K-means / EM still feel shaky.
- Optional: **@neuer2024machine** Ch 5 for an engineering perspective on unsupervised methods.

**Next week (Unit 6):** transfer learning and data scarcity — the supervised counterpart to today's unsupervised toolkit.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
**Set expectations for the exercise.** Part 1 (clustering) is the must-finish; Part 2 (conv-AE) is the new-content stretch; Part 3 (anomaly detection) is the reach goal. Students who finish 1 in 30 min and reach for 2 will grasp this lecture's content much better than those who only do 1 carefully.

**Hand-off cues.** "The synthetic data, the Conv-AE skeleton, and a notebook scaffold are on the course Git. Read the Sandfeld + Goodfellow chapters *before* the exercise — they save you 30 min of head-scratching during it."

**Reading priorities.** If they only read one thing this week: Sandfeld Ch 11 — it ties everything in §A–C to materials examples in the same vocabulary we used today. If they read two: + Goodfellow Ch 14 §14.1–14.3 — the canonical AE chapter, very readable. If three: + Bishop §9.1–9.2 for the EM derivation if it's still shaky from MFML W5.

**Closing sentence.** "Next week: transfer learning. The supervised counterpart to today. Plus: how to use the AE features we learned today as a starting point for *small-label* supervised models. The two halves of the unsupervised/supervised story will close." End there. Take questions.
:::
